title: Refueling Ban Introduced
summary: From 2010, refueling during the race is banned. Cars must start with a full fuel tank, fundamentally changing race strategy and making qualifying pace less representative of race performance.
detail: The refueling ban led to significant changes in car design, team strategy, and the nature of racing. Cars became much heavier at the start, affecting tire wear and weight distribution dynamics throughout the race.
source_url: Refueling banned — cars must start with full fuel load

title: New Points System Introduced
summary: A new points system has been introduced: 25-18-15-12-10-8-6-4-2-1 for the top ten positions. This replaces the old 10-8-6-5-4-3-2-1 system, increasing the gap between the winner and the podium finishers.
detail: The new points system aims to increase the value of victories and create a greater difference between first place and subsequent positions. It also extends the points-scoring zone to tenth place instead of eighth.

title: Q3 Tire Rule Implemented
summary: A new rule has been introduced: drivers who advance to the third qualifying segment (Q3) must start the race on the tires with which they set their fastest qualifying lap.
detail: This rule adds a strategic element to qualifying, forcing teams to balance quick lap times with optimal tire choice for the race start.
source_url: Q3 tyre rule — must start race on qualifying tyres

title: Grid Expanded to 26 Cars
summary: The grid has been expanded from 20 to 26 cars with the arrival of three new teams: Lotus Racing, Virgin Racing, and HRT. This is the largest field since 1995.
detail: The grid expansion resulted from the FIA's initiative to reduce costs and attract new participants. Three new teams were granted entry to Formula 1 with the condition of adhering to budget restrictions.
source_url: Grid expanded to 26 cars with three new teams

title: Voluntary KERS Ban Agreed
summary: Teams reached a voluntary agreement not to use the Kinetic Energy Recovery System (KERS) in the 2010 season to reduce costs and level the playing field with the new teams.
detail: Although KERS is technically permitted by the regulations, all teams agreed not to use it in 2010. This decision was made to help the new teams and reduce costs amid the economic crisis.
source_url: Teams agree to voluntary KERS ban for 2010

title: Fuel Tanks Enlarged
summary: Fuel tanks have been increased by 22 centimeters in length to accommodate fuel for the entire race without refueling. Maximum capacity is now approximately 240 liters.
detail: The increase in fuel tank size required significant changes to chassis design. The additional fuel weight at the start substantially affects handling and tire wear in the first part of the race.
source_url: Fuel tank capacity increased by 22cm for no-refueling era

title: Front Tire Width Reduced
summary: Front tire width has been reduced from 270mm to 245mm to decrease grip and compensate for the absence of refueling, which makes cars heavier at the start.
detail: Narrower front tires were introduced to maintain car balance given the significantly increased starting weight due to the refueling ban. This also helps control cornering speeds.
source_url: Front tyre width reduced from 270mm to 245mm

title: Minimum Weight Increased
summary: The minimum car weight has been increased from 605kg to 620kg without fuel, to compensate for the refueling ban and the additional structural weight required for larger fuel tanks.
detail: The minimum weight increase makes it easier for teams to reach the weight limit given the reinforced structure for larger fuel tanks and the inability to shed fuel weight during the race.
source_url: Minimum car weight increased from 605kg to 620kg

title: Michael Schumacher Returns with Mercedes GP
summary: Seven-time world champion Michael Schumacher returns to Formula 1 after three years away, joining the new Mercedes GP team as Nico Rosberg's teammate.
detail: Schumacher's return was one of the biggest sensations of the off-season. The 41-year-old German decided to come out of retirement to help Mercedes GP in their return to Formula 1 as a works team after purchasing Brawn GP.
source_url: Michael Schumacher returns to F1 with Mercedes GP

title: Jenson Button Joins McLaren
summary: Reigning world champion Jenson Button leaves Brawn GP (now Mercedes) and joins McLaren, where he will partner Lewis Hamilton, creating one of the strongest driver lineups on the grid.
detail: Button signed a three-year contract with McLaren following his triumphant 2009 season. The union of two British world champions in one team promises an exciting intra-team rivalry.
source_url: Jenson Button joins McLaren as reigning World Champion

title: Fernando Alonso Joins Ferrari
summary: Two-time world champion Fernando Alonso fulfills a childhood dream by joining Ferrari after four years at Renault. The Spaniard signed a three-year contract with the Scuderia.
detail: Alonso's move to Ferrari was the culmination of lengthy negotiations. The Spaniard had always dreamed of racing for the legendary Italian team and now has the opportunity to lead their championship assault.
source_url: Fernando Alonso signs with Ferrari

title: Nico Rosberg Joins Mercedes GP
summary: Nico Rosberg leaves Williams and joins Mercedes GP, where he will partner Michael Schumacher. For the German, this is an opportunity to race for his country's works team.
detail: Rosberg spent four successful seasons at Williams, but the move to a works Mercedes team opens new prospects. Partnering with the legendary Schumacher will be an important test for the young German.
source_url: Nico Rosberg joins Mercedes GP alongside Schumacher

title: Rubens Barrichello Moves to Williams
summary: Veteran Rubens Barrichello leaves Brawn GP (where he finished runner-up in 2009) and joins Williams, where he will team up with rookie Nico Hulkenberg.
detail: The 38-year-old Brazilian with a record 300+ Grand Prix starts brings enormous experience to Williams. His task is to help the team return to fighting for top positions and mentor his young teammate.
source_url: Rubens Barrichello moves to Williams

title: Robert Kubica Joins Renault
summary: Robert Kubica leaves BMW Sauber (following BMW's withdrawal from Formula 1) and joins Renault as team leader. The Pole gets the opportunity to lead the French team's project.
detail: Kubica became Renault's main target after Alonso's departure. The quick and talented Pole proved his speed at BMW and now gets the chance to become the undisputed team leader with ambitious plans.
source_url: Robert Kubica joins Renault F1

title: Vitaly Petrov — First Russian in Formula 1
summary: Vitaly Petrov becomes the first Russian driver in Formula 1 history, signing with Renault. The 25-year-old rookie attracted attention with victories in GP2.
detail: Petrov rose through karting and various European series to the GP2 Asia championship and a strong season in the main GP2 series. His arrival at Renault opens a new chapter for Russian motorsport in the pinnacle of racing.
source_url: Vitaly Petrov becomes first Russian F1 driver at Renault

title: Nico Hulkenberg — Williams Rookie
summary: 2009 GP2 champion Nico Hulkenberg secures a seat at Williams as Rubens Barrichello's teammate. The German rookie is considered one of the most promising young talents.
detail: Hulkenberg dominated GP2 in 2009 and earned his promotion to Formula 1. Williams see him as a potential future champion, and partnering with Barrichello should accelerate his development.
source_url: Nico Hulkenberg joins Williams as rookie

title: Felipe Massa Returns After Injury
summary: Felipe Massa returns to Ferrari after a serious head injury sustained in qualifying at the 2009 Hungarian Grand Prix. The Brazilian missed the second half of last season.
detail: Massa's recovery from a skull fracture was a medical miracle. After months of rehabilitation, the Brazilian was cleared to race and is ready to return to Ferrari, where he will now partner Fernando Alonso.
source_url: Felipe Massa returns to Ferrari after skull injury

title: Lotus Racing Enters Formula 1
summary: Lotus Racing becomes one of three new teams in Formula 1. The Malaysian team revives the legendary Lotus name, though it has no direct connection to the original Team Lotus.
detail: Lotus Racing was founded by a Malaysian consortium and obtained a license to use the Lotus name. The team is based in Norfolk, UK, and plans gradual development with the goal of scoring points in the future.
source_url: Lotus Racing enters F1 as new constructor

title: Virgin Racing Enters Formula 1
summary: Virgin Racing debuts in Formula 1 under the leadership of British billionaire Richard Branson. The team became the first to design a car entirely using CFD without physical wind tunnel testing.
detail: Virgin Racing uses a revolutionary approach to car design, relying entirely on computational fluid dynamics (CFD) without using a wind tunnel. This bold experiment aims to demonstrate the capabilities of modern technology.
source_url: Virgin Racing enters F1 with CFD-only designed car

title: HRT Enters Formula 1
summary: Hispania Racing Team (HRT) becomes the third new team of the 2010 season, representing Spain in Formula 1. The team faced serious financial difficulties even before the season began.
detail: HRT was founded by former driver Adrian Campos with backing from Spanish investors. The team experienced enormous difficulties preparing for the season and barely managed to build a car in time for the first race.
source_url: HRT (Hispania Racing) enters F1

title: Toyota Leaves Formula 1
summary: Toyota announces its immediate withdrawal from Formula 1 despite a competitive 2009 season where the team finished fifth in the Constructors' Championship. The departure is linked to the global financial crisis.
detail: Toyota's decision shocked the Formula 1 world. The Japanese manufacturer spent billions over eight seasons but never won a single race. The economic crisis forced Toyota management to reconsider participation in the expensive Formula 1 program.
source_url: Toyota withdraws from F1 despite competitive 2009 season

title: BMW Exits — Sauber Returns
summary: BMW announces its withdrawal from Formula 1 and sells the team back to Peter Sauber. The team returns to its original Sauber name and will use Ferrari engines.
detail: BMW spent four seasons in Formula 1 as a team owner, peaking in 2008 with Kubica's first victory. However, the financial crisis and a disappointing 2009 led to the decision to withdraw. Peter Sauber bought back the team and restored the historic name.
source_url: BMW exits F1, team reverts to Sauber under Peter Sauber

title: Bruno Senna Leaves HRT
summary: Bruno Senna, nephew of the legendary Ayrton Senna, left HRT after 14 races of the 2010 season due to the team's financial problems and lack of sponsorship backing.
detail: Bruno Senna started the season with HRT, but the team's chronic financial problems and lack of competitiveness led to his mid-season departure. The Brazilian sought opportunities with a more stable team.
source_url: Bruno Senna leaves HRT mid-season

title: Karun Chandhok Leaves HRT
summary: Indian driver Karun Chandhok was replaced at HRT mid-season after 13 races. His seat was taken by a more experienced driver with better funding.
detail: Chandhok was one of HRT's first drivers, but the team's financial difficulties required bringing in drivers with sponsorship packages. Despite solid performances in difficult conditions, the Indian lost his seat.
source_url: Karun Chandhok replaced at HRT

title: Sakon Yamamoto Debuts at HRT
summary: Japanese driver Sakon Yamamoto secured a seat at HRT as a replacement for one of the main drivers, bringing the team necessary funding and test driver experience.
detail: Yamamoto previously raced in Formula 1 for Spyker and Super Aguri, and was also a test driver for Renault. His experience and sponsorship backing made him an attractive candidate for the struggling HRT.
source_url: Sakon Yamamoto joins HRT as replacement driver

title: Christian Klien Replaces at HRT
summary: Austrian driver Christian Klien returned to Formula 1 with HRT as a replacement. The experienced driver previously raced for Jaguar, Red Bull, and Force India.
detail: Klien left Formula 1 at the end of 2008 but got the opportunity to return with HRT thanks to his experience and willingness to help the young team. His knowledge was valuable for car development.
source_url: Christian Klien replaces at HRT

title: Pedro de la Rosa Leaves Sauber
summary: Pedro de la Rosa was replaced at Sauber from the 14th round of the season by Nick Heidfeld. The Spaniard started the season with the team but failed to deliver the expected results.
detail: De la Rosa spent the first half of the season at Sauber after the team's return under Peter Sauber's ownership, but insufficient results led to the decision to replace him with the more experienced Heidfeld for the final part of the championship.
source_url: Pedro de la Rosa replaced at Sauber from Round 14

title: Nick Heidfeld Joins Sauber
summary: Experienced German driver Nick Heidfeld replaced Pedro de la Rosa at Sauber from the Singapore Grand Prix (14th round). Heidfeld spent the rest of the season helping the team with development.
detail: Heidfeld missed the start of the 2010 season after leaving BMW, but got the chance to return with Sauber. His extensive experience and technical knowledge were important for the team in adapting after regaining independent status.
source_url: Nick Heidfeld joins Sauber replacing de la Rosa

title: Four Drivers Can Still Win the Championship
summary: With one race remaining, Alonso leads Webber by 8 points, Vettel by 15, and Hamilton by 24. The 2010 Abu Dhabi Grand Prix will decide the most dramatic title fight in years.
detail: Heading into the season finale at Yas Marina, Fernando Alonso (246 pts) holds the advantage over Mark Webber (238), Sebastian Vettel (231), and Lewis Hamilton (222). Alonso needs only a P2 finish to guarantee the title regardless of other results. This is the first time since 2003 that four drivers enter the final race with a realistic chance of winning the championship.
